tool_calls.name: get_graph_schema
tool_calls.result_preview:
# AAS Graph Schema

All nodes also carry the label `GraphNode`.

## Core Nodes

### AssetAdministrationShell
The top-level AAS entity.
- Labels: AssetAdministrationShell, Identifiable, Referable, Refe

args.cypher:
MATCH (sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
WHERE sc.id IN ['https://admin-shell.io/dfki/SubmodelTemplate/FacilityInformation/1/0', 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/smt/assetlocation/1/0']
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm)
RETURN aas.idShort, aas.id, sm.idShort, sm.id, sc.id AS templateId

args.name: FacilityInformation

args.cypher:
MATCH (sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/HierarchicalStructures/1/1/Submodel'})
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm)
RETURN aas.idShort, aas.id, sm.idShort, sm.id

MATCH (sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
RETURN DISTINCT sc.id ORDER BY sc.id

args.name: FacilityInformation

tool_calls.name: get_template

args.name: HierarchicalStructures

args.name: Hierarchical Structures enabling Bills of Material

args.cypher:
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
RETURN aas.idShort, aas.id, sm.idShort, sm.id, sc.id AS templateSemanticId

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {id: 'urn:aas:hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'HierarchicalStructures'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
RETURN el.idShort, labels(el) AS types, el.smId
LIMIT 50

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {id: 'urn:aas:hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'HierarchicalStructures'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(parent:Entity)-[:HAS_ELEMENT]->(child:Entity)
MATCH (child)-[:REPRESENTS_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
RETURN child.idShort, asset.globalAssetId, asset.assetKind

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {id: 'urn:aas:hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'HierarchicalStructures'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:Entity)
WHERE el.idShort IN ['UR20_001', 'MiR250_002', 'UR3e_001', 'MiR250_001']
MATCH (el)-[:REPRESENTS_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
RETURN el.idShort, asset.globalAssetId, asset.assetKind

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {id: 'urn:aas:hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'FacilityInformation'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
RETURN el.idShort, el.value, el.valueType, labels(el) AS types

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {id: 'urn:aas:hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
RETURN sm.idShort, sm.id

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:MANAGES_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
WHERE aas.idShort IN ['UR20_001', 'MiR250_002', 'UR3e_001', 'MiR250_001']
RETURN aas.idShort, aas.id, asset.globalAssetId, asset.assetKind

args.name: Hierarchical Structures enabling Bills of Material

args.cypher:
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
RETURN aas.idShort, aas.id, sm.idShort, sm.id, sc.id AS templateSemanticId
LIMIT 50

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: "Hall3"})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
RETURN sm.idShort, sm.id

MATCH (sm:Submodel {id: "urn:submodel:hall3:hierarchicalstructures"})-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
RETURN el.idShort, labels(el) AS types, el.smId
LIMIT 100

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: "Hall3"})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: "HierarchicalStructures"})
      -[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(device:Entity)
WHERE device.idShort IN ["UR20_001", "MiR250_002", "UR3e_001", "MiR250_001"]
RETURN device.idShort, device.entityType

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:MANAGES_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
WHERE aas.idShort IN ["UR20_001", "MiR250_002", "UR3e_001", "MiR250_001"]
RETURN aas.idShort, aas.id, asset.globalAssetId, asset.assetKind

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: "Nameplate"})
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(prop:Property)
WHERE aas.idShort IN ["UR20_001", "MiR250_002", "UR3e_001", "MiR250_001"]
RETURN aas.idShort, prop.idShort, prop.value

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: "TechnicalData"})
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(prop:Property)
WHERE aas.idShort IN ["UR20_001", "MiR250_002", "UR3e_001", "MiR250_001"]
RETURN aas.idShort, prop.idShort, prop.value

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE aas.idShort IN ["UR20_001", "MiR250_002", "UR3e_001", "MiR250_001"]
RETURN aas.idShort, sm.idShort, sm.id

MATCH (sm:Submodel {id: "urn:submodel:hall3:facilityinformation"})-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
RETURN el.idShort, el.modelType, el.value

MATCH (sm:Submodel {id: "urn:submodel:hall3:facilityinformation"})-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(mlp:MultiLanguageProperty)
WHERE mlp.idShort = "PrimaryUse"
MATCH (mlp)-[:HAS_VALUE]->(ls:LangString)
RETURN mlp.idShort, ls.language, ls.text

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: "TechnicalData"})
WHERE aas.idShort IN ["UR20_001", "MiR250_002", "UR3e_001", "MiR250_001"]
RETURN aas.idShort, sm.id

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE aas.idShort IN ["UR20_001", "MiR250_002", "UR3e_001", "MiR250_001"]
RETURN aas.idShort, sm.idShort, sm.id

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: "Nameplate"})
      -[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(prop:Property)
WHERE aas.idShort IN ["UR20_001", "MiR250_002", "UR3e_001", "MiR250_001"]
RETURN aas.idShort, prop.idShort, prop.value

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: "Nameplate"})
      -[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(prop:Property)
WHERE aas.idShort IN ["UR20_001", "MiR250_002", "UR3e_001", "MiR250_001"]
AND prop.idShort = "URIOfTheProduct"
RETURN aas.idShort, prop.value

Now I need to find their locations. Let me check if they have location information. I'll look for submodels that might contain location data. Let me first check what submodels each instance has.

No location submodel found with that template. Let me check if there's location information stored elsewhere - perhaps in the Nameplate submodel or as properties within the AAS. Let me look at the Nameplate submodel contents for each robot.









The Nameplate submodel doesn't contain location information. Let me check if there's any other submodel that might contain location data. Let me also check the TechnicalDataAGV submodel for each robot to see if it contains location info.

The Nameplate submodel doesn't have location info. Let me check if there's any other submodel that might contain location information. Let me also check if there's a FacilityInformation submodel that might contain location data.









I see there are Hall3 and Hall4 AAS with FacilityInformation submodels. These might contain location information. Let me check the FacilityInformation submodel contents to see if they contain robot location data.









The FacilityInformation submodel contains hall information (area, power connection) but not robot locations. Let me check if there's a relationship between the robot AAS and the hall AAS. Let me also check if there's any other submodel that might contain location data for the robots.

So each robot has a Nameplate submodel and MiR100_001 has many ServiceRequestNotification submodels. Let me check the Nameplate submodels for location information. I'll look at the elements within the Nameplate submodels.









The Nameplate submodels contain serial numbers, dates, and URIs, but no location information. Let me check if there's any location information in the TechnicalDataAGV submodels, or if there's a separate location submodel that I might have missed.

Let me try a different approach - let me check all submodels in the system to see if any have location-related semanticIds:









I see there are FacilityInformation submodels for hall3 and hall4. Let me check if the robots are associated with these halls. Let me also look at the TechnicalDataAGV submodels to see if they contain location information.

The instance shells don't have TechnicalDataAGV submodels - only the type shells do. Let me check the FacilityInformation submodels to see if they contain location data that might be associated with the robots.

# AAS Graph Schema

All nodes also carry the label `GraphNode`.

The top-level AAS entity.

args.cypher:
MATCH (sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/SubmodelTemplate/technicaldataagv/1/0'})
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm)
RETURN aas.idShort AS shellName, aas.id AS aasId, sm.idShort AS submodelName, sm.id AS submodelId

MATCH (instance:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:DERIVED_FROM]->(type:AssetAdministrationShell)
WHERE type.idShort IN ['MiR250_Type', 'MiR100_Type']
RETURN instance.idShort AS robotName, instance.id AS aasId, type.idShort AS typeShell

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE aas.id IN ['urn:aas:mir100:001', 'urn:aas:mir250:002', 'urn:aas:mir250:001']
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
RETURN aas.idShort, sm.idShort, sm.id, sc.id AS templateSemanticId

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE aas.id IN ['urn:aas:mir100:001', 'urn:aas:mir250:002', 'urn:aas:mir250:001']
AND sm.idShort = 'TechnicalDataAGV'
RETURN aas.idShort, sm.id, sm.idShort

MATCH (sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
WHERE sc.id = 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/smt/assetlocation/1/0'
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm)
RETURN aas.idShort, sm.idShort, sm.id

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'MiR100_001'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'Nameplate'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
RETURN el.idShort, el.modelType, el.value, el.idShortPath
LIMIT 20

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE aas.id IN ['urn:aas:mir100:001', 'urn:aas:mir250:002', 'urn:aas:mir250:001']
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
RETURN aas.idShort, sm.idShort, sm.id, sc.id AS templateId

MATCH (sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
WHERE sc.id = 'https://admin-shell.io/dfki/SubmodelTemplate/FacilityInformation/1/0'
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm)
RETURN aas.idShort, sm.idShort, sm.id

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:MANAGES_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
WHERE aas.id IN ['urn:aas:mir100:001', 'urn:aas:mir250:002', 'urn:aas:mir250:001']
RETURN aas.idShort, asset.globalAssetId, asset.assetKind, asset.assetType

MATCH (sm:Submodel {id: 'urn:submodel:hall4:facilityinformation'})-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
RETURN el.idShort, el.modelType, el.value, el.idShortPath
LIMIT 30

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE aas.id IN ['urn:aas:mir100:001', 'urn:aas:mir250:002', 'urn:aas:mir250:001']
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(rel:RelationshipElement)
RETURN aas.idShort, rel.idShort, rel.idShortPath

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'MiR100_001'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(p:Property)
RETURN p.idShort, p.value, p.idShortPath

args.cypher:

RETURN sm.idShort, sm.id

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)

LIMIT 30

MATCH (sm:Submodel {id: 'urn:submodel:hall4:hierarchicalstructures'})-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
RETURN el.idShort, el.modelType, el.idShortPath
LIMIT 30

MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
RETURN el.idShort, el.value, el.idShortPath

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall4'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'FacilityInformation'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
RETURN el.idShort, el.value, el.idShortPath

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'HierarchicalStructures'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
RETURN el.idShort, el.value, el.idShortPath
LIMIT 30

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall4'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'HierarchicalStructures'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
RETURN el.idShort, el.value, el.idShortPath
LIMIT 30

tool_calls.name: get_graph_schema
tool_calls.result_preview:
# AAS Graph Schema

All nodes also carry the label `GraphNode`.

## Core Nodes

### AssetAdministrationShell
The top-level AAS entity.
- Labels: AssetAdministrationShell, Identifiable, Referable, Refe

args.cypher:
MATCH (sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/SubmodelTemplate/technicaldataagv/1/0'})
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm)
RETURN aas.idShort AS shellName, aas.id AS aasId, sm.idShort AS submodelName, sm.id AS submodelId

MATCH (instance:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:DERIVED_FROM]->(type:AssetAdministrationShell)
WHERE type.idShort IN ['MiR250_Type', 'MiR100_Type']
RETURN instance.idShort AS robotName, instance.id AS aasId, type.idShort AS typeShell

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE aas.id IN ['urn:aas:mir100:001', 'urn:aas:mir250:002', 'urn:aas:mir250:001']
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
RETURN aas.idShort, sm.idShort, sm.id, sc.id AS templateSemanticId

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE aas.id IN ['urn:aas:mir100:001', 'urn:aas:mir250:002', 'urn:aas:mir250:001']
AND sm.idShort = 'TechnicalDataAGV'
RETURN aas.idShort, sm.id, sm.idShort

MATCH (sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
WHERE sc.id = 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/smt/assetlocation/1/0'
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm)
RETURN aas.idShort, sm.idShort, sm.id

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'MiR100_001'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'Nameplate'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
RETURN el.idShort, el.modelType, el.value, el.idShortPath
LIMIT 20

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE aas.id IN ['urn:aas:mir100:001', 'urn:aas:mir250:002', 'urn:aas:mir250:001']
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
RETURN aas.idShort, sm.idShort, sm.id, sc.id AS templateId

MATCH (sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
WHERE sc.id = 'https://admin-shell.io/dfki/SubmodelTemplate/FacilityInformation/1/0'
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm)
RETURN aas.idShort, sm.idShort, sm.id

MATCH (sm:Submodel {idShort: 'FacilityInformation'})-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
WHERE sm.id IN ['urn:submodel:hall4:facilityinformation', 'urn:submodel:hall3:facilityinformation']
RETURN sm.id AS submodelId, el.idShort, el.value, el.idShortPath
LIMIT 30

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE aas.idShort IN ['MiR100_001', 'MiR250_001', 'MiR250_002']
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
WHERE el.modelType = 'Property' OR el.modelType = 'MultiLanguageProperty'
RETURN aas.idShort, sm.idShort, el.idShort, el.value
LIMIT 50

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'MiR100_001'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'Nameplate'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(p:Property)
RETURN p.idShort, p.value, p.valueType

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:MANAGES_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
WHERE aas.idShort IN ['MiR100_001', 'MiR250_001', 'MiR250_002']
RETURN aas.idShort, asset.globalAssetId, asset.assetKind, asset.assetType

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE aas.idShort IN ['MiR100_001', 'MiR250_001', 'MiR250_002']
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(rel:RelationshipElement)
RETURN aas.idShort, rel.idShort, rel.idShortPath

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)
WHERE aas.idShort IN ['Hall3', 'Hall4', 'MiR100_001', 'MiR250_001', 'MiR250_002']
RETURN aas.idShort, aas.id

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'MiR100_001'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
WHERE el.idShort IN ['Location', 'Position', 'Hall', 'Area', 'Building']
RETURN el.idShort, el.value, el.idShortPath, el.modelType

MATCH (aas1:AssetAdministrationShell)-[r]->(aas2:AssetAdministrationShell)
WHERE aas1.idShort IN ['MiR100_001', 'MiR250_001', 'MiR250_002']
RETURN aas1.idShort, type(r), aas2.idShort

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
WHERE el.modelType = 'Property' OR el.modelType = 'MultiLanguageProperty'
RETURN el.idShort, el.value, el.idShortPath

MATCH (sm:Submodel {idShort: 'FacilityInformation'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
RETURN sm.idShort, el.idShort, el.modelType, el.value, el.idShortPath

tool_calls.name: query_aas_graph

args.cypher:
MATCH (sm:Submodel)
WHERE sm.idShort CONTAINS 'Location' OR sm.idShort CONTAINS 'Position' OR sm.idShort CONTAINS 'Hall'
RETURN sm.idShort, sm.id

tool_calls.result_preview: {"error":"forbidden_pattern","violations":[{"rule":"idShort_contains_or_regex","hint":"Use exact match instead: WHERE aas.idShort = 'ExactName'. See cypher.md anti-pattern #4.","match":"sm.idShort CON

args.cypher:
MATCH (sm:Submodel)
RETURN sm.idShort, sm.id

MATCH (sm:Submodel {idShort: 'HierarchicalStructures'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
RETURN sm.idShort, el.idShort, el.modelType, el.value, el.idShortPath
LIMIT 30

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'HierarchicalStructures'})
WHERE aas.idShort IN ['Hall3', 'Hall4']
RETURN aas.idShort, sm.id

MATCH (sm:Submodel {idShort: 'HierarchicalStructures'})-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
WHERE el.idShort IN ['MiR100_001', 'MiR250_001', 'MiR250_002']
MATCH (sm)<-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]-(aas:AssetAdministrationShell)
RETURN aas.idShort AS hallName, sm.id AS submodelId, el.idShort AS robotName

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'FacilityInformation'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
WHERE el.idShort IN ['Area', 'PowerConnection', 'PrimaryUse']
RETURN el.idShort, el.value

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall4'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'FacilityInformation'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
WHERE el.idShort IN ['Area', 'PowerConnection', 'PrimaryUse']
RETURN el.idShort, el.value

- MiR250_001 (derived from MiR250_Type)

I see the robots have Nameplate submodels and many ServiceRequestNotification submodels, but I don't see an Asset Location submodel yet. Let me check if there's an Asset Location submodel by looking for the "Data Model for Asset Location" template, or check the TechnicalDataAGV submodel for location information.

Interesting - the instance shells don't have a TechnicalDataAGV submodel. Let me check if there's an Asset Location submodel or if location data is stored elsewhere. Let me search for any submodels with "Location" in the name or check for the AssetLocation template:









No Asset Location submodels found. Let me check the Nameplate submodel for each robot to see if it contains location information:









The Nameplate doesn't contain location. Let me check if there's a FacilityInformation submodel that might contain location data, or look for any other submodels that might store location:









I see the robots only have Nameplate and ServiceRequestNotification submodels. Let me check if there's a FacilityInformation submodel that might contain location data for the facility where these robots are located. Let me also check if there's any other submodel type that might contain location:









I found FacilityInformation submodels for Hall4 and Hall3. Let me check if the robots are associated with these halls. Let me look at the FacilityInformation submodel to see what data it contains, and also check if there's a relationship between the robots and the halls:









The FacilityInformation submodels contain hall data (Area, PowerConnection) but no PrimaryUse value. Now I need to find where the robots are located. Let me check if there's a relationship between the robot AAS and the hall AAS, or if there's a location property somewhere:









The query is returning null values. Let me check the actual structure of the Nameplate submodel more carefully to see what properties it has:









Let me check the actual elements in the Nameplate submodel without filtering by modelType:









The Nameplate doesn't have location data. Let me check if there's a relationship between the robot AAS and the hall AAS. Let me also check if there's any other submodel that might contain location information. Let me look at all submodels across all AAS to see if there's a pattern:









I can see the structure now. The robots (MiR100_001, MiR250_001, MiR250_002) only have Nameplate and ServiceRequestNotification submodels. There's no explicit location submodel for the robots.

Let me check if there's a HierarchicalStructures submodel that might contain location information, or if the robots are associated with halls through some other mechanism. Let me also check the Hall AAS to see if they have references to the robots:









Excellent! I found the location information. The Hall3 HierarchicalStructures submodel contains references to:
- UR20_001
- MiR250_002
- UR3e_001
- MiR250_001

So MiR250_001 and MiR250_002 are located in Hall3. Let me check Hall4 to see if MiR100_001 is there:









I found it! MiR100_001 is located in Hall4 (under EntryNode.MiR100_001).

Now let me check Hall3 to confirm the location of MiR250_001 and MiR250_002:









Perfect! I now have all the information I need:

**Autonomous Transport Robots (AGVs) and their locations:**

1. **MiR100_001** - Located in **Hall4**
2. **MiR250_001** - Located in **Hall3**
3. **MiR250_002** - Located in **Hall3**

Let me verify this information by checking the TechnicalDataAGV submodel to confirm these are indeed autonomous transport robots:









Confirmed! The MiR100_Type and MiR250_Type shells have TechnicalDataAGV submodels, which confirms these are autonomous transport robots (AGVs).

## Summary

tool_calls.name: get_graph_schema
tool_calls.result_preview:
# AAS Graph Schema

All nodes also carry the label `GraphNode`.

## Core Nodes

### AssetAdministrationShell
The top-level AAS entity.
- Labels: AssetAdministrationShell, Identifiable, Referable, Refe

args.cypher:
MATCH (sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/SubmodelTemplate/technicaldataagv/1/0'})
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm)
RETURN aas.idShort AS shellName, aas.id AS aasId, sm.idShort AS submodelName, sm.id AS submodelId

MATCH (instance:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:DERIVED_FROM]->(type:AssetAdministrationShell)
WHERE type.idShort IN ['MiR250_Type', 'MiR100_Type']
RETURN instance.idShort AS robotName, instance.id AS aasId, type.idShort AS typeShell

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE aas.id IN ['urn:aas:mir100:001', 'urn:aas:mir250:002', 'urn:aas:mir250:001']
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
RETURN aas.idShort, sm.idShort, sm.id, sc.id AS templateSemanticId

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE aas.id IN ['urn:aas:mir100:001', 'urn:aas:mir250:002', 'urn:aas:mir250:001']
AND sm.idShort = 'TechnicalDataAGV'
RETURN aas.idShort, sm.id, sm.idShort

MATCH (sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
WHERE sc.id = 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/smt/assetlocation/1/0'
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm)
RETURN aas.idShort, sm.idShort, sm.id

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'MiR100_001'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'Nameplate'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
RETURN el.idShort, el.modelType, el.value, el.idShortPath
LIMIT 20

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE aas.id IN ['urn:aas:mir100:001', 'urn:aas:mir250:002', 'urn:aas:mir250:001']
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
RETURN aas.idShort, sm.idShort, sm.id, sc.id AS templateId

MATCH (sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
WHERE sc.id = 'https://admin-shell.io/dfki/SubmodelTemplate/FacilityInformation/1/0'
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm)
RETURN aas.idShort, sm.idShort, sm.id

MATCH (sm:Submodel {idShort: 'FacilityInformation'})-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
WHERE sm.id IN ['urn:submodel:hall4:facilityinformation', 'urn:submodel:hall3:facilityinformation']
RETURN sm.id AS submodelId, el.idShort, el.value, el.idShortPath
LIMIT 30

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE aas.idShort IN ['MiR100_001', 'MiR250_001', 'MiR250_002']
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
WHERE el.modelType = 'Property' OR el.modelType = 'MultiLanguageProperty'
RETURN aas.idShort, sm.idShort, el.idShort, el.value
LIMIT 50

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'MiR100_001'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'Nameplate'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
WHERE el.modelType IN ['Property', 'MultiLanguageProperty']
RETURN el.idShort, el.value, el.valueType, el.idShortPath

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'MiR100_001'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'Nameplate'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
RETURN el.idShort, el.modelType, el.value, el.idShortPath

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
RETURN aas.idShort, sm.idShort, sm.id
ORDER BY aas.idShort, sm.idShort

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'HierarchicalStructures'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
WHERE el.modelType IN ['SubmodelElementCollection', 'SubmodelElementList', 'Entity']
RETURN aas.idShort, sm.id, el.idShort, el.modelType
LIMIT 30

MATCH (sm:Submodel {idShort: 'HierarchicalStructures'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
RETURN sm.id AS submodelId, el.idShort, el.modelType, el.value
LIMIT 50

Interesting - the instance shells don't have a TechnicalDataAGV submodel. Let me check if there's an Asset Location submodel or if location data is stored elsewhere. Let me search for any submodels with "Location" in the name or check for the AssetLocation template:









No Asset Location submodels found. Let me check the Nameplate submodel for each robot to see if it contains location information:









The Nameplate doesn't contain location. Let me check if there's a FacilityInformation submodel that might contain location data, or look for any other submodels that might store location:









I see the robots only have Nameplate and ServiceRequestNotification submodels. Let me check if there's a FacilityInformation submodel that might contain location data for the facility where these robots are located. Let me also check if there's any other submodel type that might contain location:









I found FacilityInformation submodels for Hall4 and Hall3. Let me check if the robots are associated with these halls. Let me look at the FacilityInformation submodel to see what data it contains, and also check if there's a relationship between the robots and the halls:

Excellent! I found the location information. The Hall3 HierarchicalStructures submodel contains references to:
- MiR250_002
- MiR250_001

Let me also check Hall4 to see if any robots are located there:









I found that MiR100_001 is located in Hall4 (under EntryNode). Let me also check Hall3 to see if any robots are there:









I found the locations of all the autonomous transport robots:

**From Hall3 HierarchicalStructures:**
- MiR250_002
- MiR250_001

**From Hall4 HierarchicalStructures:**
- MiR100_001

Now let me verify this information and provide a complete answer. Let me also check if there are any other AGVs I might have missed by looking at all AAS with TechnicalDataAGV submodels:









Good - I've confirmed there are only 2 type shells (MiR250_Type and MiR100_Type) and I found all 3 instance shells (MiR100_001, MiR250_001, MiR250_002).

Let me now provide a summary of the findings:

   - Year of Construction: 2020

2. **MiR250_001** (MiR250 model)
   - Location: **Hall3**

3. **MiR250_002** (MiR250 model)
   - Location: **Hall3**

According to the graph data, the autonomous transport robots are located as follows:
- **Hall3**: MiR250_001 and MiR250_002
- **Hall4**: MiR100_001

The location information is stored in the HierarchicalStructures submodel of each hall, which contains the EntryNode with references to the assets located in that hall.

result.query: Which transport robot can carry more than 200 kg?
result.response:
I need to find transport robots in the AAS system and check their payload capacities. Let me start by exploring the graph structure to understand how transport robots are represented.









Now let me get the templates index to understand what templates are available for finding transport robots and their specifications.









I can see there's a "Technical Data for Automated Guided Vehicles" template which is relevant for transport robots (AGVs). Let me first find all AAS that have submodels with this template, then check their payload specifications.









Good! I found two AGV type shells: MiR250_Type and MiR100_Type. Now I need to get the template structure to understand what elements contain the payload information.









Perfect! I can see that `MaxLoadMass` is a Property element within the `TechnicalParameters` collection. Now let me query the actual values for both MiR250 and MiR100 to find their maximum load masses.









Excellent! I found the payload capacities:
- **MiR100_Type**: 100 kg
- **MiR250_Type**: 250 kg

Now let me also check if there are any instance shells (actual robots) derived from these type shells, as the user might be asking about actual deployed robots.

















Now I have a complete picture. Let me verify the payload capacity for the instance shells as well to ensure they inherit the same specifications.









The instance shells don't have their own TechnicalDataAGV submodels - they inherit from the type shells. This is expected behavior in the AAS architecture.

## Summary

Based on the graph data:

**Transport robots that can carry more than 200 kg:**

- **MiR250** (both type and instances MiR250_001, MiR250_002): **250 kg** maximum load capacity

**Transport robots that cannot carry more than 200 kg:**

- **MiR100** (type and instance MiR100_001): **100 kg** maximum load capacity

**Answer:** Only the **MiR250** transport robot can carry more than 200 kg (specifically 250 kg). The MiR100 model has a maximum payload of 100 kg.

I found emergency stop information, but it's from other assets (MiR100 and CRX10iA), not from the MiR250. The MiR250's HandoverDocumentation submodel has `not_indexed` status, meaning its PDF documents were never ingested into the search index.

Let me check if there are any other submodels in the MiR250 that might have indexed content, or if there's any other documentation available.

The diagnostic confirms `not_indexed` - the PDF document for the MiR250 has not been ingested into the document store.

Let me check if there's any other documentation available by looking at the TechnicalDataAGV submodel or any other submodels that might contain safety information.

- When the person/object is out of the safety zone, the emergency stop resets automatically
- MiR100 has a total redundant electrical safety circuit including Sick Safety components
- If internal errors occur in the safety circuit, the robot immediately goes into emergency stop (all moving parts become voltage-free by mechanical disconnection)
- Emergency stop can only be reset when redundancy is provided

**Note:** This information is from the MiR100 manual, not the MiR250. The MiR250 manual would need to be re-ingested into the vector store to provide specific emergency stop information for that model.

result.response:
I need to find the AAS for the "mir 250" asset first, then locate its documentation and search for emergency stop information.

Let me start by querying the graph to find the mir 250 asset.









No exact match. Let me try a broader search to find assets with "mir" in their idShort.









I need to use exact match. Let me first get the templates index and then try to discover what assets exist.









Now let me try to find all AAS shells to see what's available, and look for one that might be "mir 250".









Found it! There are MiR250 shells:
- MiR250_001 (instance)
- MiR250_002 (instance)
- MiR250_Type (type)

Let me query the type shell first to find documentation, as documentation typically lives on the Type AAS.









Good! I found submodels. The "HandoverDocumentation" submodel likely contains the operator manual. Let me search for emergency stop information in that submodel's documents.









The submodel is not indexed. Let me check if there are any PDF files in the HandoverDocumentation submodel.









Found a PDF file. Now I need to find the submodel_id to search the documents. Let me get the submodel that contains this file.









Now I have the submodel_id. Let me search the PDF documentation for emergency stop information.









The document is not indexed. Let me check if there are other submodels or if the PDF is in a different location. Let me also check the instance shell (not just the type shell).









Let me check if there's an instance shell derived from this type, and also look for all submodels in this AAS.









Let me check if there's an instance shell and also search the TechnicalDataAGV submodel for any documentation.









Good, there are instance shells. Let me check if the instance shells have the same submodels and if the PDF is accessible there.









The instance doesn't have the PDF. Let me check if the HandoverDocumentation submodel has any other elements that might be documents or if there's a different way to access the content.









I see there's a "DigitalFiles" element which is a File. Let me check if the PDF is actually indexed. The diagnostic said "not_indexed" which means the PDF was never ingested into the document store.

Let me try searching without a submodel_id to see if there's any indexed content at all, or check if there are other submodels with documents.









The PDF exists but is not indexed. Let me try searching the MiR100 documentation to see if it's indexed, to understand the pattern.









Good! The MiR100 documentation is indexed and contains emergency stop information. The MiR250 documentation is not indexed (as we saw earlier with "not_indexed" diagnostic).

Let me try searching without the submodel_id to see if there's any indexed content for MiR250 at all.









The search results don't contain MiR250-specific content - they're from CRX10iA documentation. The MiR250 PDF is not indexed in the document store.

Let me check if there's any other information about emergency stops in the TechnicalDataAGV submodel or other submodels that might contain this information.









The TechnicalDataAGV submodel contains technical parameters but no emergency stop information. Let me check the Nameplate submodel for any safety-related information.









The Nameplate submodel doesn't contain emergency stop information. Let me check the TechnicalDataAGV submodel.









The TechnicalDataAGV submodel contains technical parameters but no emergency stop information. The HandoverDocumentation submodel has the PDF file, but it's not indexed in the document store.

Let me verify this by checking if there's any indexed content for the MiR250 submodel at all.









The MiR250 documentation is confirmed as **not indexed**. The PDF file exists in the HandoverDocumentation submodel but has not been ingested into the document search index.

Let me provide a summary of what I found:

## Summary

Based on my investigation of the AAS data:

**What I found:**
- The MiR250 asset has an AAS (Asset Administration Shell) with ID `urn:aas:mir250:type` (type shell) and instances `MiR250_001` and `MiR250_002`
- The HandoverDocumentation submodel (`urn:submodel:mir250:type:handoverdocumentation`) contains a PDF file at `/aasx/files/MiR250.pdf`

**What I could NOT find:**
- The MiR250 PDF documentation is **not indexed** in the document search system (diagnostic: "not_indexed")
- Therefore, I cannot search the operator manual for emergency stop information

**Comparison with MiR100:**
- The MiR100 documentation IS indexed and contains emergency stop information (safety circuit details, reset procedures, etc.)
- This suggests the MiR250 PDF exists but hasn't been processed for document search

**Conclusion:**
According to the graph data, the MiR250 operator manual PDF exists in the HandoverDocumentation submodel, but it has not been ingested into the document search index. I cannot provide specific emergency stop information from the manual because the document is not searchable.

**Recommendation:** The PDF file needs to be ingested into the document store to enable searching for emergency stop procedures and safety information.

The documentation mentions emergency stop functionality and safety standards (EN 13850 for emergency stop), but doesn't have a specific error code table for the red status light. However, based on the error code "E-STOP-ACTIVATED" and the red status light, I can provide the following information:

**According to the graph data:**
- The MiR100_001 transport robot in Hall 4 has an active service request notification
- The error code is "E-STOP-ACTIVATED" with High priority
- The status is "Open"

**General guidance (based on standard industrial robot safety):**
A red status light with E-STOP-ACTIVATED error code typically means:
- The emergency stop button has been pressed or triggered
- The safety circuit is open
- The robot is in a safe stopped state

**How to fix it:**
1. **Check for the emergency stop button** - Look for a red mushroom-shaped E-stop button on the robot (typically on the top module or control box)
2. **Release the emergency stop** - Twist or pull the E-stop button to release it
3. **Check for safety obstacles** - Ensure no objects are blocking the robot's path or triggering safety sensors
4. **Reset the system** - After releasing the E-stop, the robot may need to be reset through the MiR Web Interface
5. **Check the MiR Web Interface** - Go to Service > Status > Diagnostics to verify all components are OK

**According to the manual (page 20):**
- Before first drive, test the vehicle via MiR Web Interface: Service > Status > Diagnostics
- Check that each sub-component works and status should be green and read OK

**Next steps:**
1. Physically check the robot for any pressed emergency stop buttons
2. Release any triggered E-stop buttons
3. Access the MiR Web Interface to reset the system
4. Verify the status light turns green and the error clears

If the issue persists after these steps, contact technical support as there may be a deeper safety system issue.

Let me list all submodels of MiR100_001 and look for ones that might contain status information. I'll check the TechnicalData template which might have status info.









I see only Nameplate and ServiceRequestNotification submodels. Let me check one of the ServiceRequestNotification submodels to see if it contains status information about the red light.









I found an error code "E-STOP-ACTIVATED" with High priority and CorrectiveMaintenance service type. This might be related to the red status light. Let me check if there's documentation about this error code. First, let me look for PDF documents related to this submodel.









The submodel doesn't have indexed documents. Let me check the Nameplate submodel for any documentation or look for a TechnicalData submodel that might have status information. Let me also check if there's a TechnicalDataAGV template that might contain status info.









Let me check the MiR250 robots to see if they have more submodels that might contain status information.









Let me check the MiR250_002 robot as well.









It seems the MiR robots only have Nameplate submodels. Let me check if there's a TechnicalData submodel in the Hall4 AAS that might contain status information about the robots.









Let me check the TechnicalData template to see if there's a submodel with that template. Let me also search for any submodel that might have status or error information by looking at all submodels in the graph.









Let me check if there are any PDF documents in the system that might contain information about the red status light. Let me search for documents related to MiR robots.









I found some relevant information. I see a document about MiR100 type with information about "lost" alert and yellow-purple error lights. Let me search more specifically for red status light information in the MiR100 type documentation.









I found information about the safety circuit and emergency stop. The document mentions that when the safety circuit is triggered, the robot goes into emergency stop. Let me search for more specific information about the red status light and how to reset it.









Let me check the "5.4 Start the vehicle" section which might have information about resetting the emergency stop.









Based on my research, I found the following information:

1. **The transport robot in Hall 4 is MiR100_001** (confirmed from the Hall4 HierarchicalStructures submodel)

2. **The red status light means the emergency stop (E-STOP) has been activated** - This is confirmed by the ServiceRequestNotification submodel which shows:
   - Error Code: `E-STOP-ACTIVATED`
   - Priority: `High`
   - Service Type: `CorrectiveMaintenance`

3. **According to the MiR100 documentation (Handover Documentation submodel):**
   - The safety circuit forces the robot into emergency stop when a person or object enters the safety zones
   - When the safety circuit is triggered, "all moving parts will be voltage free by mechanical disconnection"
   - The emergency stop can be reset when the redundancy is provided

4. **How to fix it:**
   - First, check if there's a person or object in the robot's safety zone
   - Remove any obstacles from the safety zone
   - The emergency stop should reset automatically when the person/object is out of the safety zone
   - If there's an internal error in the safety circuit, you may need to manually reset the emergency stop

Let me provide a concise answer to the user.

---

**The red status light on the MiR100 transport robot in Hall 4 means the emergency stop (E-STOP) has been activated.**

**What it means:**
- The robot's safety circuit has triggered an emergency stop
- All moving parts are voltage-free (mechanically disconnected)
- This is a high-priority safety condition

**How to fix it:**

1. **Check the safety zone** - Look for any person or object that may have entered the robot's safety area
2. **Remove obstacles** - Clear any objects or people from the robot's path and safety zone
3. **Wait for automatic reset** - According to the MiR100 manual, the emergency stop resets automatically when the person/object is out of the safety zone
4. **If the issue persists** - There may be an internal safety circuit error. Check the MiR Web Interface:
   - Log on to the MiR Web Interface
   - Go to Service > Status > Diagnostics
   - Check component status and look for any error messages

**Source:** According to the graph data, the ServiceRequestNotification shows error code `E-STOP-ACTIVATED` with High priority. The MiR100 Handover Documentation (page 10) states that the safety circuit forces emergency stop when safety zones are breached, and the robot stops immediately with all moving parts voltage-free.

**How to fix it:**
According to general MiR robot knowledge (since the documentation doesn't have specific red light troubleshooting):

1. **Check for emergency stop activation** - Look for the emergency stop button on the robot (usually on the top module or emergency stop box)
2. **Release the emergency stop** - Twist or pull the emergency stop button to release it
3. **Reset the robot** - After releasing the E-STOP, the robot may need to be restarted or reset through the MiR Control app
4. **Check for obstructions** - Ensure there are no obstacles triggering the safety system
5. **Verify the robot can resume operation** - Check if the robot returns to normal operation

**Important safety notes:**
- This is a **High priority** issue requiring immediate attention
- The emergency stop is a safety feature that stops the robot immediately
- Before resetting, ensure the area around the robot is clear and it's safe to resume operation
- If the E-STOP keeps activating, there may be an underlying issue that needs technical support

**Status Light Information:**
According to the MiR100 User Guide (page 14), the robot uses **yellow-purple error lights** to indicate a 'lost' situation - when the vehicle cannot find a safe path to its destination. The robot stops and activates these lights.

- `E-STOP-ACTIVATED` 
- `EmergencyStop`

**To Fix:**

2. **For Emergency Stop**: If the emergency stop is activated, release it and restart the vehicle
3. **For 'Lost' Alert**: Check the map and ensure the robot can find a safe path to its destination
4. **Contact Support**: If the issue persists, contact Mobile Industrial Robots at support@mir-robots.com or +45 20 377 577

**General guidance:** For MiR robots, a red light typically indicates an emergency stop or critical fault condition. Check the emergency stop button on top of the robot and ensure it's released.

result.query: The transport robot in Hall 4 has a red status light — what does it mean and how do I fix it?
result.response:
I need to find the transport robot in Hall 4, check its status, and look up the meaning of the red status light in the documentation.

Let me start by finding the transport robot in Hall 4.









I need to use exact matches. Let me first get the templates index and then search for assets in Hall 4.









Now let me search for all AAS shells to find the transport robot in Hall 4. I'll list all shells first to see what's available.









I see there's a "Hall4" AAS and several MiR (Mobile Industrial Robot) shells. MiR robots are transport robots. Let me check the Hall4 AAS to see what submodels it has, and also check the MiR robots to find which one is in Hall 4.









Let me check the MiR robots to see which one is associated with Hall 4. I'll look at the MiR250_001 and MiR250_002, MiR100_001 to find the one in Hall 4.









I see many MiR robots. Let me check the Hall4 AAS more thoroughly to see if there's a relationship to a specific robot, or check the FacilityInformation submodel which might contain location info.









Let me check the Nameplate submodel of Hall4 to see if it identifies which robot is in Hall 4.









The Hall4 AAS represents the production hall itself, not a robot. Let me check the MiR robots to see which one is in Hall 4. I'll look at the MiR250_001 and MiR250_002 nameplates to find location info.









Let me check the MiR250_002 and also look for any location-related submodels in the MiR robots.









Let me try a different approach - check all MiR robots individually.









I see MiR250_001, MiR250_002, and MiR100_001. Let me check the TechnicalDataAGV submodel of the MiR250_Type to understand the robot status indicators, and also check if there's a status submodel in the instance shells.

Let me first check the MiR250_001 for any status-related submodels:









Let me check MiR250_002:









Let me check the MiR100_001 which has many ServiceRequestNotification submodels. This might be the robot with issues. Let me also check if there's a TechnicalDataAGV submodel that might contain status information.









I see MiR100_001 has many ServiceRequestNotification submodels. Let me check the MiR250_Type and MiR100_Type to see if they have TechnicalDataAGV submodels that might contain status information.









Let me check the TechnicalDataAGV submodel of MiR250_Type to see if it contains status information:









I don't see status information here. Let me check the HandoverDocumentation submodel which might contain PDF documentation about status lights:









There's a PDF file. Let me search for information about red status light in this PDF. But first, I need to find which MiR robot is in Hall 4. Let me check if there's a relationship between the Hall4 AAS and any MiR robot.









I found it! The Hall4 AAS has a HierarchicalStructures submodel that contains "MiR100_001" as an EntryNode. So the transport robot in Hall 4 is **MiR100_001**.

Now let me search for information about the red status light in the MiR100 documentation. First, let me check if MiR100_Type has a HandoverDocumentation with a PDF:









Now let me search the MiR100.pdf for information about red status light:









I found some information about alerts and error lights. Let me search more specifically for red light status:









I found a reference to "yellow-purple error lights" but not specifically about red light. Let me search for more specific information about status lights and their meanings:









I found a reference to "LED color light bar (on four sides): Indication of operating status" but no specific color meanings. Let me search for troubleshooting information:









I found a reference to "yellow-purple error lights" for when the vehicle is "lost". Let me check the MiR250 documentation as well, since the user mentioned "transport robot" which could be either MiR100 or MiR250. Let me also check if there's any status information in the ServiceRequestNotification submodels of MiR100_001:









I see there are ServiceRequestNotifications with error codes like "E-STOP-ACTIVATED" and "EmergencyStop". This suggests the robot may have been stopped due to an emergency stop being activated.

Let me check the MiR250 documentation for status light information:









The MiR250 documentation doesn't have indexed chunks. Let me check if there's a TechnicalDataAGV submodel for MiR100_Type that might have status information:









Based on my research, I found:

1. **The transport robot in Hall 4 is MiR100_001** (confirmed from the HierarchicalStructures submodel of Hall4)

2. **From the MiR100 documentation**, I found:
   - The robot has an "LED color light bar (on four sides): Indication of operating status"
   - When the vehicle is "lost" (cannot find a safe path), it turns on "yellow-purple error lights"
   - There are ServiceRequestNotifications with error codes "E-STOP-ACTIVATED" and "EmergencyStop"

3. **However, the specific meaning of a RED status light is not documented** in the available PDF documentation for MiR100.

Let me provide the user with what I found and note that the specific red light meaning is not in the documentation:

---

**Based on the graph data and documentation:**

The transport robot in Hall 4 is **MiR100_001** (Mobile Industrial Robot 100).

**What I found about status lights:**
- The MiR100 has an LED color light bar on all four sides that indicates operating status
- According to the MiR100 manual, when the robot is "lost" (cannot find a safe path to its destination), it displays **yellow-purple error lights** and stops
- The robot currently has active Service Request Notifications with error codes: **E-STOP-ACTIVATED** and **EmergencyStop**

**Regarding the RED status light:**
The specific meaning of a **red status light** is **not documented** in the available MiR100 PDF documentation. The manual only mentions yellow-purple lights for the "lost" condition.

**Recommended actions:**
1. **Check if the emergency stop was activated** - The robot has active E-STOP error notifications. Press the emergency stop reset button (usually on the top module) if it was triggered
2. **Consult the manufacturer** - Contact Mobile Industrial Robots support at:
   - Phone: +45 20 377 577
   - Email: support@mir-robots.com
   - Website: www.mir-robots.com
3. **Check the MiR250 documentation** - If this is actually a MiR250 robot, the status light meanings may differ

**General guidance:** Red lights on industrial robots typically indicate:
- Emergency stop activation
- Critical fault requiring immediate attention
- Safety system triggered

Please verify the exact robot model (MiR100 vs MiR250) and contact the manufacturer for the specific red light meaning for your unit.

For complete troubleshooting guidance, refer to the MiR100 user manual (MiR100.pdf) in the HandoverDocumentation submodel.

records.query: The transport robot in Hall 4 has a red status light — what does it mean and how do I fix it?

result.query: The transport robot in Hall 4 has a red status light — what does it mean and how do I fix it?
result.response:
I need to find the transport robot in Hall 4, check its status, and look up the meaning of the red status light in the documentation.

Let me start by finding the transport robot in Hall 4.









I need to use exact matches. Let me first get the templates index and then search for assets in Hall 4.









Now let me search for all AAS shells to find the transport robot in Hall 4. I'll list all shells first to see what's available.









I see there's a "Hall4" AAS and several MiR (Mobile Industrial Robot) shells. MiR robots are transport robots. Let me check the Hall4 AAS to see what submodels it has, and also check the MiR robots to find which one is in Hall 4.









Let me check the MiR robots to see which one is associated with Hall 4. I'll look at the MiR250_001 and MiR250_002, MiR100_001 to find the one in Hall 4.









I see many MiR robots. Let me check the Hall4 AAS more thoroughly to see if there's a relationship to a specific robot, or check the FacilityInformation submodel which might contain location info.









Let me check the Nameplate submodel of Hall4 to see if it identifies which robot is in Hall 4.









The Hall4 AAS represents the production hall itself, not a robot. Let me check the MiR robots to see which one is in Hall 4. I'll look at the MiR250_001 and MiR250_002 nameplates to find location info.









Let me check the MiR250_002 and also look for any location-related submodels in the MiR robots.









Let me try a different approach - check all MiR robots individually.









I see MiR250_001, MiR250_002, and MiR100_001. Let me check the TechnicalDataAGV submodel of the MiR250_Type to understand the robot status indicators, and also check if there's a status submodel in the instance shells.

Let me first check the MiR250_001 for any status-related submodels:









Let me check MiR250_002:









Let me check the MiR100_001 which has many ServiceRequestNotification submodels. This might be the robot with issues. Let me also check if there's a TechnicalDataAGV submodel that might contain status information.









I see MiR100_001 has many ServiceRequestNotification submodels. Let me check the TechnicalDataAGV submodel of MiR250_Type to understand the status indicators and what a red light means.









I don't see status information here. Let me check the HandoverDocumentation submodel which might contain troubleshooting info or status light meanings.









I see there's a PDF file at `/aasx/files/MiR250.pdf`. Let me search for information about red status light in this document. But first, I need to find the submodel_id for the HandoverDocumentation.









Now let me search the PDF documentation for information about red status light:









The document is not indexed. Let me check if there's a TechnicalData submodel or any other submodel that might contain status information. Let me also check the MiR100_Type for TechnicalDataAGV:









I don't see status light information in the TechnicalDataAGV. Let me check the HandoverDocumentation submodel which might contain troubleshooting information:









I found a PDF file reference: `/aasx/files/MiR250.pdf`. Let me search this document for information about red status light. First, I need to get the submodel_id for the HandoverDocumentation:









The document is not indexed. Let me check if there's a different submodel that might contain status information. Let me look for any submodel that might have status or light information by checking all submodels across all AAS shells:









Let me check the ServiceRequestNotification submodels to see if any of them contain information about a red status light issue:









Let me check the content of one of these ServiceRequestNotification submodels to see if they contain status information:









I see there's an error code "E-STOP-ACTIVATED" in one of the ServiceRequestNotifications. This might be related to the red status light. Let me check more ServiceRequestNotification submodels to find one that mentions a red status light:









Let me check the MiR250_001 and MiR250_002 to see if they have any status information. Also, let me check if there's a relationship between Hall4 and any of the MiR robots:









I see that MiR100_001 has many ServiceRequestNotification submodels. Let me check the content of a few more to find one that mentions a red status light. Let me also check the HierarchicalStructures submodel of Hall4 to see if it contains information about which robot is in Hall 4:









I found it! The HierarchicalStructures submodel of Hall4 shows that **MiR100_001** is in Hall 4. Now I need to find information about the red status light. Let me check the ServiceRequestNotification submodels of MiR100_001 to see if any of them contain information about a red status light issue.

Let me check a few more ServiceRequestNotification submodels to find one that mentions a red status light:









I see an error code "EmergencyStop" in this ServiceRequestNotification. Let me check more ServiceRequestNotification submodels to find one that specifically mentions a red status light:









Let me check the HandoverDocumentation submodel of MiR100_Type to see if it contains troubleshooting information about status lights:









I see there's a PDF file `/aasx/files/MiR100.pdf` for the MiR100. Let me search this document for information about red status light. First, I need to get the submodel_id for the HandoverDocumentation of MiR100_Type:









Now let me search the MiR100 PDF for information about red status light:









I found some information about alerts and error lights, but I need to find specific information about the red status light. Let me search for more specific terms like "red light" or "status light":









I found a reference to the emergency stop box in the document. Let me search for more specific information about the status light colors and their meanings:









I found a reference to "yellow-purple error lights" for the 'lost' alert. Let me search for more specific information about the red status light and emergency stop:









Based on my research, I found that:

1. **The transport robot in Hall 4 is MiR100_001** (confirmed from the HierarchicalStructures submodel of Hall4)

2. **From the ServiceRequestNotification submodels**, I found error codes like "E-STOP-ACTIVATED" and "EmergencyStop" which indicate the emergency stop has been activated.

3. **From the MiR100 user guide PDF**, I found references to:
   - Emergency stop box that must be properly mounted
   - Yellow-purple error lights for 'lost' alerts
   - The document mentions the emergency stop system

However, I haven't found specific information about what a **red status light** means in the available documentation. Let me check if there's more specific information about status light colors in the MiR250 documentation as well:









The MiR250 documentation is not indexed. Based on my findings, I can provide the following answer:

---

**According to the graph data:**

The transport robot in Hall 4 is **MiR100_001** (confirmed from the Hall4 HierarchicalStructures submodel).

**From the ServiceRequestNotification records**, I found that this robot has multiple open service requests with error codes including:
- **E-STOP-ACTIVATED** (Emergency Stop activated)
- **EmergencyStop**

**What the red status light likely means:**

Based on the MiR100 user guide documentation and the error codes found:

1. **Red status light typically indicates an Emergency Stop has been activated** - This is a safety-critical state where the robot has been stopped due to the emergency stop button being pressed or the emergency stop circuit being triggered.

2. **The emergency stop box must be properly mounted** - According to the MiR100 manual, the emergency stop box must be removed and remounted on the top module in a position where it can be reached from all sides.

**How to fix it:**

1. **Check the emergency stop button** - Look for the emergency stop button on the robot and verify if it has been pressed. If pressed, twist or pull to release it.

2. **Check the emergency stop circuit** - Ensure all emergency stop devices in the system are properly connected and not triggered.

3. **Reset the emergency stop** - Once the emergency stop is released, the robot should be able to resume normal operation.

4. **Verify the top module mounting** - Ensure the emergency stop box is properly mounted and accessible.

**According to the ServiceRequestNotification records**, there are already open service requests for this robot with "High" priority and "CorrectiveMaintenance" service type, indicating this is a known issue that needs attention.

**Note:** If the red light persists after releasing the emergency stop, or if you cannot locate the emergency stop button, contact technical support as there may be a deeper issue with the emergency stop circuit or sensor system.

According to the MiR100 User Guide documentation:

2. **Turn the black On/Off button** on the front of the vehicle

4. **Reset the emergency stop** - Release the emergency stop button
5. **Turn the robot back on** using the On/Off button

LIMIT 20

Based on the graph data and documentation, here's what I found:

## What the red status light means

The **red status light on the MiR100_001 transport robot in Hall 4 indicates that the Emergency Stop has been activated**. This is confirmed by the ServiceRequestNotification records which show error codes like:
- "Emergency Stop - Red LED ON"
- "E-STOP-ACTIVATED"
- "Emergency Stop Activated"

The priority is marked as **High** and the service type is **CorrectiveMaintenance**.

## How to fix it

According to the MiR100 User Guide (page 19, section 5.4 "Start the vehicle"), you need to:

1. **Check the emergency stop button** - Locate and verify the emergency stop button on the robot
2. **Reset the emergency stop** - Turn the emergency stop button to release it
3. **Start the vehicle** - Follow the startup procedure in the MiR Web Interface

The documentation also mentions (page 19, section 5.3) that the emergency stop box must be properly mounted and accessible from all sides of the module.

**General guidance:** If the emergency stop cannot be reset or the red light persists after resetting, contact your retailer or Mobile Industrial Robots support at http://mobile-industrial-robots.com/en/retailers/ (page 25, section 9 Service).

The ServiceRequestNotification submodels show this issue has been logged with High priority, so you may want to check if there are any additional service requests or if maintenance has already been scheduled.

args.cypher:
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
WHERE aas.idShort CONTAINS 'Hall 4' OR aas.idShort CONTAINS 'transport' OR aas.idShort CONTAINS 'robot'
RETURN aas.idShort, aas.id, sm.idShort, sm.id, sc.id AS templateSemanticId
LIMIT 20

Based on the graph data, the transport robot in Hall 4 is **MiR100_001**.

**What the red status light means:**
According to the ServiceRequestNotification records in the AAS, a red status light indicates **"Emergency Stop - Red LED ON"** or **"E-STOP-ACTIVATED"**. This is a high-priority fault that requires corrective maintenance.

**How to fix it:**
1. **Check the emergency stop button** on the robot - it may have been pressed accidentally or intentionally
2. **Reset the emergency stop** by turning the button clockwise and releasing it
3. **Power cycle the robot** if needed - turn it off and back on
4. **Clear any error codes** from the robot's control panel
5. **Verify the robot is ready** to resume operations

**Important:** The emergency stop is a safety feature. Before resetting, ensure:
- No personnel are in the robot's path
- The area is clear of obstacles
- The robot's path is safe for operation

If the red light persists after resetting the emergency stop, there may be an underlying issue that requires technical support.

records.query: Which of our robots can carry the heaviest load?

result.query: Which of our robots can carry the heaviest load?
result.response:
I need to find information about robot load capacities in the AAS system. Let me start by exploring the graph structure to understand how robot data is organized.









Now let me get the templates index to understand what templates are available for robot specifications:









I see there's a "Technical Data" template and "TechnicalDataAGV" for AGVs. Let me first explore what submodels exist in the graph to understand the structure better. I'll look for submodels that might contain robot technical data.









Good! I can see there are submodels with "TechnicalDataAGV" and "Nameplate" templates. Let me also check for the "TechnicalData" template (0173-1#01-AHX837#002) which might be used for general technical data. Let me query for all submodels and their semanticIds to get a complete picture:









I can see several robot types:
- CRX10iA (Universal Robots)
- MiR100, MiR250 (Mobile Industrial Robots - AGVs)
- UR20, UR3e (Universal Robots)

I see there are "TechnicalData" and "TechnicalDataAGV" submodels. Let me check the structure of the TechnicalData template to understand what properties it contains:









Now let me check the TechnicalDataAGV template structure:









Perfect! I can see that the TechnicalDataAGV template has a `MaxLoadMass` property under `TechnicalParameters`. Now let me query the MiR100 and MiR250 type shells to get their MaxLoadMass values:









Good! MiR100 has a max load of 100 (likely kg). Now let me check MiR250:









MiR250 has a max load of 250. Now let me check the UR robots (UR20 and UR3e) which use the "TechnicalData" template. I need to look for load capacity properties in the TechnicalData template. Let me first explore what properties exist in the TechnicalData submodels:









Excellent! I can see UR20 has a "Payload" property with value "20". Let me check UR3e:









UR3e has a payload of 3. Now let me also check the CRX10iA robot:









CRX10iA also has a TechnicalData submodel. Let me check its payload:









Now I have all the robot payload/load capacity data:

1. **MiR100** (AGV): 100 kg
2. **MiR250** (AGV): 250 kg
3. **UR20** (robot arm): 20 kg
4. **UR3e** (robot arm): 3 kg
5. **CRX10iA** (robot arm): 10 kg

Let me verify this by also checking if there are any other robots I might have missed. Let me list all AAS that might be robots:









Good, I have all the robot types. The Hall3 and Hall4 are facility AAS, not robots. Now let me compile the complete list of robot load capacities:

**Robot Load Capacities (from graph data):**
- **MiR250**: 250 kg (AGV)
- **MiR100**: 100 kg (AGV)
- **UR20**: 20 kg (robot arm)
- **CRX10iA**: 10 kg (robot arm)
- **UR3e**: 3 kg (robot arm)

The **MiR250** can carry the heaviest load at **250 kg**.

Let me provide a clear answer to the user:

According to the graph data, here are the load capacities for all robots in our system:

| Robot Model | Type | Max Load |
|-------------|------|----------|
| **MiR250** | AGV (Mobile Robot) | **250 kg** |
| MiR100 | AGV (Mobile Robot) | 100 kg |
| UR20 | Robot Arm | 20 kg |
| CRX10iA | Robot Arm | 10 kg |
| UR3e | Robot Arm | 3 kg |

**The MiR250 can carry the heaviest load at 250 kg.**

This data comes from the TechnicalDataAGV submodel for the MiR robots and the TechnicalData submodel for the Universal Robots arms.

MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(maxLoad:Property {idShort: "MaxLoadMass"})
RETURN aas.idShort AS robotType, maxLoad.value AS maxLoadMass, maxLoad.valueType

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: "UR3e_Type"})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: "TechnicalData"})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(p:Property {idShort: "Payload"})

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:MANAGES_ASSET]->(a:Asset)
RETURN aas.idShort AS aasIdShort, a.globalAssetId AS globalAssetId, a.assetKind

Now let me get the templates index to understand what templates are available for robot specifications:









I see there's a "Technical Data" template and "Technical Data for Automated Guided Vehicles" template. Let me first query the graph to find all AAS shells and their submodels to identify which ones might be robots. I'll look for submodels that might contain technical data about robots.

Good, I can see the robot AAS shells. Now I need to find the technical data submodels for each robot to get their load capacity information. Let me query for all submodels with their semanticIds to identify which ones contain technical data:









I need to use exact matches. Let me first list all AAS shells to see what robot names exist:









Good, I can see the robot AAS shells. Now let me query each robot's submodels to find the TechnicalData submodel and extract load capacity information. Let me start by checking the structure of the TechnicalData template:









The Technical_Data template has a complex structure with TechnicalPropertyAreas. Let me also check the "Technical Data for Automated Guided Vehicles" template which might have load capacity info:









I can see that the "Technical Data for Automated Guided Vehicles" template has a `MaxLoadMass` property under `TechnicalParameters`. This is likely what I need for the MiR robots (which are AGVs). 

Now let me query the graph to find the TechnicalData submodels for each robot and extract the MaxLoadMass property. Let me start by checking which submodels have the AGV technical data template:









Good, I found the AGV technical data submodels for MiR250_Type and MiR100_Type. Now let me also check if there are instance shells with this data. Let me query for all submodels with their semanticIds to find all TechnicalData submodels:









I see there are TechnicalDataAGV submodels. Now let me also check for the "Technical_Data" template (0173-1#01-AHX837#002) which might be used by UR robots:









No submodels with that exact semanticId. Let me check what semanticIds the TechnicalData submodels have for the UR robots:









The TechnicalData submodel for UR3e_Type doesn't have a semanticId. Let me check the structure of this submodel to find load capacity information:









I can see the UR3e_Type has a `Payload` property with value "3" (likely 3 kg). Now let me check the other robots. Let me query all robot AAS shells and their TechnicalData submodels to get their payload/load capacity:









Good. Now let me extract the payload/load capacity from each of these submodels. Let me start with the MiR robots (which use TechnicalDataAGV template with MaxLoadMass):









MiR100 has MaxLoadMass of 100 (likely kg). Now let me check MiR250:









MiR250 has MaxLoadMass of 250 kg. Now let me check the UR robots for Payload:









Good, UR3e has a payload of 3 kg. Now let me check the other robots. Let me query all robot AAS shells and their TechnicalData submodels to find payload/load capacity:









I need to use exact matches. Let me query each robot individually. First, let me get the MiR robots' MaxLoadMass:









MiR250 has MaxLoadMass of 250 kg. Let me check MiR100:









MiR100 has MaxLoadMass of 100 kg. Now let me check the UR robots. I already know UR3e has Payload of 3 kg. Let me check UR20:









UR20 has Payload of 20 kg. Now let me check CRX10iA:









CRX10iA has Payload of 10 kg. Now let me also check the instance shells to see if they have different values. Let me check the MiR250_001 and MiR250_002 instances:









The instance shell MiR250_001 doesn't have a TechnicalDataAGV submodel. Let me check if it has a TechnicalData submodel:









The instance shell only has a Nameplate submodel. Let me check the UR3e_001 and UR3e_002 instances:









The instance shells don't have TechnicalData submodels. Let me check the UR20_001 instance:









The instance shells only have Nameplate submodels. Let me check the CRX10iA_001 instance:









The CRX10iA_001 instance also doesn't have a TechnicalData submodel. Let me check the MiR100_001 instance:









The instance shells don't have TechnicalData submodels. The technical data is only available in the Type shells. Let me also check if there are any other robots I might have missed. Let me look at the MiR250_002 instance:









Now let me compile the results. Based on my queries, I have found the following load capacities for the robots:

1. **MiR250_Type**: MaxLoadMass = 250 kg
2. **MiR100_Type**: MaxLoadMass = 100 kg
3. **UR20_Type**: Payload = 20 kg
4. **UR3e_Type**: Payload = 3 kg
5. **CRX10iA_Type**: Payload = 10 kg

The MiR250 has the highest load capacity at 250 kg.

Let me verify this by checking if there are any other robots I might have missed. Let me also check the UR3e_002 instance to see if it has any technical data:









The instance shells don't have TechnicalData submodels. The technical data is only available in the Type shells.

Based on my analysis, I have found the following load capacities for all robots in the system:

| Robot Model | Load Capacity |
|-------------|---------------|
| MiR250_Type | 250 kg |
| MiR100_Type | 100 kg |
| UR20_Type | 20 kg |
| CRX10iA_Type | 10 kg |
| UR3e_Type | 3 kg |

**The MiR250 robot can carry the heaviest load at 250 kg.**

| CRX10iA | 10 kg |
| UR3e | 3 kg |

**The MiR250 robot can carry the heaviest load at 250 kg.**
